tex:
\begin{question*}{12}{a}Claudia asks some students to choose their favourite science from biology, chemistry and physics.
\newline
The pie chart shows the results.
\newline

\begin{InsertScreenShot}

\end{InsertScreenShot}

\begin{subpartsaliged}
Find the percentage of students who choose chemistry.
\newline

Find the fraction of students who choose physics.
\newline
Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline

For the number of students choosing each subject, find the ratio biology : chemistry : physics.
\newline
Give your answer in its simplest form.
\newline

Marcus says:

`I do not know how many people choose chemistry, but I do know it is an even number.
\newline
'

Explain how Marcus knows this.
\newline

Claudia now tells Marcus that 26 students choose chemistry.
\newline

Work out how many students choose physics.
\newline

\end{subpartsaliged}

(b)The Venn diagram shows information about the number of students in a class who study geography ($G$) and history ($H$).
\newline

\begin{InsertScreenShot}

\end{InsertScreenShot}

\begin{subpartsaliged}
Work out the number of students in the class.
\newline

Find $n(G)$.
\newline

One of the students is chosen at random.
\newline

Find the probability that this student studies geography and history.
\newline

One of the students who studies geography and history stops studying history.
\newline

Complete this Venn diagram to show this change.
\newline

\begin{InsertScreenShot}

\end{InsertScreenShot}

\end{subpartsaliged}

\end{question*}

qtype: Cost calculation, percentage discount, percentage increase
tex:
A shop sells food and drink.
\newline

(a) Bananas cost \$1.20 per kilogram and apples cost \$2.25 per bag.
\newline

Work out the total cost of 3.5 kg of bananas and 2 bags of apples.
\newline

(b) Students receive a 10\% discount on their shopping.
\newline
Before the discount, the cost of a student's shopping is \$16.80.
\newline

Work out the amount of the discount.
\newline

(c) The cost of a cabbage increases by 15\%.
\newline

Calculate the new price if the original price is \$1.80.
\newline

(d) Some customers have their shopping delivered to their home.
\newline
The cost is \$5 plus \$1.50 for each kilometre travelled from the shop to their home.
\newline

(i) (e) Show that the cost for a customer living 10 km from the shop is \$20.
\newline

(f) \begin{InsertScreenShot}

\end{InsertScreenShot}

On the grid, draw a line to show the cost of having shopping delivered.
\newline

(g) A bottle of water costs \$1.55.
\newline
Suki has \$20.
\newline

Work out the maximum number of bottles Suki can buy and the change she receives.
\newline

(h) A farmer delivers eggs to the shop in trays of 50.
\newline
The eggs are then put into boxes of 12.
\newline
There are no eggs left in the trays and all of the egg boxes are full.
\newline

Work out the smallest possible number of eggs that the farmer delivers.
\newline

(i) The shop sells bottles of orange juice in three different sizes.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{ccc}
Bottle A & Bottle B & Bottle C \\
0.5 litres & 1.2 litres & 2 litres \\
\$1.30 & \$3.20 & \$5.25 \\
\end{tabular}
Work out which bottle is the best value.
\newline
Show how you decide.
\newline

content.content:
Bananas cost \$1.20 per kilogram and apples cost \$2.25 per bag.
\newline

Work out the total cost of 3.5 kg of bananas and 2 bags of apples.
\newline

Students receive a 10\% discount on their shopping.
\newline
Before the discount, the cost of a student's shopping is \$16.80.
\newline

Work out the amount of the discount.
\newline

The cost of a cabbage increases by 15\%.
\newline

Calculate the new price if the original price is \$1.80.
\newline

Some customers have their shopping delivered to their home.
\newline
The cost is \$5 plus \$1.50 for each kilometre travelled from the shop to their home.
\newline

Show that the cost for a customer living 10 km from the shop is \$20.
\newline

On the grid, draw a line to show the cost of having shopping delivered.
\newline

A bottle of water costs \$1.55.
\newline
Suki has \$20.
\newline

Work out the maximum number of bottles Suki can buy and the change she receives.
\newline

A farmer delivers eggs to the shop in trays of 50.
\newline
The eggs are then put into boxes of 12.
\newline
There are no eggs left in the trays and all of the egg boxes are full.
\newline

Work out the smallest possible number of eggs that the farmer delivers.
\newline

The shop sells bottles of orange juice in three different sizes.
\newline

Work out which bottle is the best value.
\newline
Show how you decide.
\newline

A shop sells food and drink.
\newline

tex:
\begin{question*}{12}{a}A boat sails from A to B.
\newline
The travel graph shows this journey.
\newline

\begin{InsertScreenShot}

\end{InsertScreenShot}

\begin{subpartsaliged}
Write down the time that the boat leaves A.
\newline

Work out how long, in minutes, it takes the boat to sail from A to B.
\newline

The boat stays at B for 20 minutes.
\newline
The boat then sails to C at a constant speed of 8 km/h.
\newline

Complete the travel graph.
\newline

Work out the average speed, in km/h, for the whole journey from A to C.
\newline

\end{subpartsaliged}

(b)The scale drawing shows the positions of two ports, X and Y.
\newline
The scale is 1 cm represents 8 km.
\newline

\begin{InsertScreenShot}

\end{InsertScreenShot}

\begin{subpartsaliged}
Measure the bearing of Y from X.
\newline

A boat, B, is 52 km from X and 80 km from Y.
\newline

On the scale drawing, mark the position of B.
\newline

A ship, S, is on a bearing of 284° from X.
\newline

Work out the bearing of X from S.
\newline

\end{subpartsaliged}

\end{question*}

qtype: visual sequences, nth term, solving equations from sequences
tex:
The grid shows the first three diagrams in a sequence.
\newline
Each diagram is made using sticks.
\newline

\begin{InsertScreenShot}

\end{InsertScreenShot}

(a) On the grid, draw Diagram 4.
\newline

(b) Complete the table.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
Diagram number & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\
\hline
Number of sticks & 5 & 9 & 13 & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

(c) (i) (d) Find an expression, in terms of $n$, for the number of sticks in Diagram $n$.
\newline

(e) One of the diagrams has 73 sticks.
\newline

Work out its Diagram number.
\newline

(f) (i) (g) Show that the total number of sticks needed to make the first 3 diagrams is 27.
\newline

(h) The total number of sticks needed to make the first $k$ diagrams is $2k^2 + 3k$.
\newline

Show that this expression gives the correct total number of sticks needed to make the first 3 diagrams.
\newline

(i) Tobias wants to make the first 10 diagrams.
\newline
He has already made the first 3 diagrams.
\newline
He has 240 sticks left to make the remaining 7 diagrams.
\newline

Work out how many sticks he has left when all 10 diagrams are made.
\newline

qtype: linear and quadratic graphs, equation of line, lowest point of graph
tex:
\begin{InsertScreenShot}

\end{InsertScreenShot}

(a) Write down the equation of line $L$ in the form $y = mx + c$.
\newline

(b) (i) (c) Complete the table of values for $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
$x$ & $-2$ & $-1$ & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\
\hline
$y$ & & 1 & $-3$ & $-5$ & $-5$ & & 1 & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}

(d) On the grid, draw the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$ for $-2 \le x \le 5$.
\newline

(e) (i) (f) Write down the coordinates of the lowest point of the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$.
\newline

(g) On the grid, draw the line of symmetry of the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$.
\newline

(h) Write down the equation of the line of symmetry.
\newline

(i) Write down the coordinates of the point where line $L$ intersects the graph of $y = x^2 - 3x - 3$ for $x \ge 0$.
\newline

qtype: Limits of accuracy, unit conversion, proportional reasoning, standard form, percentage increase
tex:
\begin{question*}{9}{a}The length, $l$ m, of a piece of wire is 18.7 metres, correct to the nearest 10 centimetres.
\newline

Complete the statement about the value of $l$.
\newline

(b)850 metres of wire has a mass of 130.5 kilograms.
\newline

Work out the length of wire, in metres, that has a mass of 900 grams.
\newline

(c)Aluminium is used to make the wire.
\newline
The mass of 1 cm$^3$ of aluminium is 2.7 grams.
\newline

Work out the mass, in grams, of 6000 cm$^3$ of aluminium.
\newline
Give your answer in standard form.
\newline

(d)A 12 metre length of wire increases in length to 12.017 metres when its temperature rises.
\newline

Calculate the percentage increase in the length of the wire.
\newline

\end{question*}
